Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang often found inspiration for his “wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) from poems, idioms and pictorial signs from archaic scripts. In this painting depicting the idiom “chuanliu buxi”(meaning “endless flow”) the pictorial image for “chuan” (meaning “river”) is illustrated by a river with fish swimming in it. The use of extremely wet brushstrokes also alludes to the theme.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.After his retirement, Huang had more time for experimentations in his art. In this highly stylized depiction of a rooster, the recurring motifs create a kaleidoscopic effect, giving this work a strong design element.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.After retirement, Huang was able to devote more time to art and experimentation. This semi-abstract work is probably a derivation of the genre of “calligraphic paintings” for which he was best known.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.In the donation by Huang’s family to SAM in 2001, this was one of several abstract paintings created using Chinese ink and watercolour that exemplify Huang’s exploration into different genres.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Gods of Fu Lu Shou "Happiness, Prosperity and Longevity"
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.“Wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) are Huang’s scholarly contributions to the artistic and literary fields. The result of his research for over a decade, they display his inventiveness, especially from the 1970s. The Chinese characters of “happiness”, “prosperity” and “longevity” take center stage in this work as their symbolic or homophonic representations (peach and deer) appear in the background.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang is best known for his unique creation of “wenzi hua” (calligraphic painting) which is the amalgamation of traditional Chinese culture and calligraphy, expressed in often seemingly abstract form. Utilizing the pictorial form of a character, Huang created a new script that allows for different presentations of a single character; in the SAM collection there are two calligraphic paintings of the character “longevity”.

A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.The Chinese characters “guangming zhengda” for the idiom “fair and square” are written in what Huang termed “wenzi hua” (calligraphic painting). “Wenzi hua” are Huang’s scholarly contributions to the artistic and literary fields and are the result of his research for over a decade. They display his inventiveness as they are derived from archaic scripts and pictorial forms.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Untitled (Calligraphy with a Man in the Middle of the Character)
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang had always been interested in Chinese etymology and his wealth of knowledge in calligraphy eventually led to the creation of “wenzi hua” (calligraphic painting). They are the result of a decade of research and display his inventiveness, as one of its unique characteristics is the reversal of roles whereby the painting supports the calligraphy-dominated work.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s calligraphic paintings are often inspired by Tang and Song poems and idioms. This is a poem by a monk named Zeng Jiaoran (730-799), describing the carefree life of a recluse. In this work, the unique feature of Huang’s distinctive script in which the painting supports the calligraphy-dominated work is clearly evident.

In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s works are often inspired by Tang and Song poems and idioms. This well-known poem, “Ascending the Stork Tower” is by Wang Zhihuan (688-742), a poet from the Tang dynasty. There is another illustration of the same poem by Huang in the SAM collection.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.“Wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) are the fruit of Huang’s research in calligraphic scripts for over a decade and they display his inventiveness, especially from the 1970s. The Chinese character for “tolerance” is written in a round, interlocking manner (starkly different from the way it is conventionally written) to give it a strong design element.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang is best known for his unique creation of “wenzi hua”(calligraphic painting) which is the amalgamation of traditional Chinese culture and calligraphy, expressed in often seemingly abstract form. He took great delight in studying the ancient forms of Chinese characters before deconstructing the different elements and recombining them in a new manner, like the character “gao” for “tall”, where the top portion of the character is reshaped to look like a roof.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: All within the Four Seas are One Family
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Not only was Huang familiar with the developments in Chinese calligraphy he was also knowledgeable about Japanese calligraphy and those of other cultures. Writing in what appears to be “One Stroke Calligraphy”, this work exemplifies Huang’s creativity as he takes elements of each character and incorporates them into the next.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Children were one of Huang’s major subjects as their innocence reminded him of his youth; he also felt less constrained when painting the liveliness and exuberance of children at play. Here, a child participates in a lion dance, playing the role of a jester taunting a northern lion (distinguished by its long coat; southern lions have a short coat due to the warmer climate).

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.One of Huang’s favourite subjects since the early days of drawing comics in the 1930s was of children playing. Executed in both Chinese ink and watercolour, this painting shows a group of highly spirited children, playing and jumping as tigers would. Titled “Hu Yue Tu” (translated as “Leaping Tigers”), this work signifies blessings for the coming year to be energetic and fruitful.

The three children here are depicted playing with fire crackers, a common activity to usher in the lunar new year.

He also felt less constrained when depicting the antics of children as seen in this painting of two children lying on a banana leaf.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s interest in religion and philosophy often translated into paintings of reflective and ephemeral themes such as depicted here. The inscription, which is also the title, asks, “Who is the one that will appreciate me?” corresponds with the illustration of two birds, each facing a different direction, searching for the one who would appreciate them.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.This is a fine example of Huang’s mastery of the ink medium and his technical virtuosity in the naturalistic and representational genre. A variety of brushstrokes from fine outlines to vigorous bold ones are employed to depict the range of textures found in the delicate flowers and leaves to the luxuriant shiny feathers of the rooster.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.In China, painting on porcelain wares (such as vases and plates), like ink painting, has a long tradition, with flowering blooms as a popular subject due to their decorative element. This work cleverly brings together the two art practices as Huang paints a painting of a vase painted with flowers.

Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Traces of Huang’s early career as a cartoonist can be detected in this painting of an extremely likeable deer, which is rendered in a naïve manner with simple outline.

In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang, who often drew inspiration from Buddhism, Confucianism and Taoism for his art, inscribed the teachings of Reverend Fa Yuan of the Song dynasty, on this painting of fantastically shaped towering peaks. The inscription states the Buddhist belief that one is born into this world with nothing and will depart with nothing.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Untitled (Calligraphy with a Man on the Left)
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s “wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) are the result of a decade of research. They display his inventiveness, especially from the 1970s, as one of its characteristics is the reversal of roles of characters and images: the former dominates while the latter tends to be small, faint or remain in the background.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: At Dusk I leave the Hills Behind, the Moon Escorts me all the way
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang was well versed in Chinese culture and literature and often drew inspiration from them for his art. This painting illustrates the poem by Tang dynasty poet Li Bai that describes one’s reluctance to return from the mountains as the sun sets and the moon rises.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Following in the tradition of Chinese landscape painting where the significance of man is downplayed, Huang’s magnificent landscapes often contain human figures so small that they are easily missed. The influence of Mi Fu (1051-1107), a Song dynasty calligrapher and painter, is seen here especially in the use of extremely wet and diffused brushwork known as “Mi dots”.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Although schooled in the art of calligraphy, Huang was exposed to Chinese ink painting through studying his family’s collection of artworks. Following in that tradition, the white spaces represent land, water and sky.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s landscapes are heavily influenced by Mi Fu (1051-1107), a Song dynasty calligrapher and painter famous for the “Mi dots” which are ink diffusions caused by extremely wet brushstrokes on paper. In the tradition of Chinese landscape painting, the emphasis is on the vast mountain while the human figure is rendered insignificant.

A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s landscape paintings are heavily influenced by Mi Fu (1051-1107), one of the four great calligraphers of the Song dynasty, whose landscape paintings are known for their misty and atmospheric qualities. Inspiration also came to Huang from poems such as those by poet Li Shangyin (c. 813-858) of the Tang dynasty.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Besides Chinese culture and literature, Huang also drew inspiration from Buddhism, Confucianism and Taoism for his art. “Mountains Filled with Buddhist Chants” illustrates a paradisiacal landscape where a small dwelling is set against a backdrop of lofty peaks. Heavily influenced by Song dynasty calligrapher and painter Mi Fu (1051-1107), Huang’s landscape paintings often take on the subject of hills shrouded in mist, executed with extremely wet brushstrokes.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Like his paintings of children, Huang’s figures in his paintings of mythology and folk tales display a playfulness that is not found in traditional renderings or in those by his contemporaries. Here the door-god Shu Bao, also known as God of Protection, is depicted looking quite benevolent which is not the conventional way of representing the fierce deity. In the SAM collection is the companion door-god Jing De, also by Huang. Images of the pair are often pasted on main entrance doors to symbolize their protection over the inhabitants of the house.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Another genre in Huang’s repertoire is the painting of folklore and mythology. He often painted familiar themes and subjects seen in traditional art, such as the popular God of Longevity. However, Huang was not content with mere replication and sought to discover new presentations. Even when comparing this work with another by him in the SAM collection of the same deity, there are marked differences. The deity here is almost recognizable, and is identified only by his characteristic high forehead and the peach in the corner.

Huang’s whimsical depiction of Zhuge Liang, the famed advisor to Liu Bei, a warlord in the classic novel “Romance of the Three Kingdoms”, shows him at ease in Longzhong, his native village.

Huang eases the solemnity of the subject with the inclusion of the child

This painting shows one of the Eight Immortals, Tie Guai Li (meaning “Li with the iron crutch”, in Chinese), who is lame and is always shown with the walking aid and a gourd in his hand or slung over his shoulder.

Contentment is a recurring theme that is seen in many of Huang’s works and here sitting under the magnificent tree is an elderly recluse, meditating in nature.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Another genre in Huang’s repertoire is the painting of folklore and mythology. Popularly known as “Great Granduncle”, the deity depicted here is a protector of seafarers as they journey to different parts of the world. Worshipped by the Chinese immigrant communities in Southeast Asia, Huang would certainly have known about this deity. With fine, controlled lines, he sketched a dignified and benevolent figure looming large over a junk, alluding to the deity’s association with travellers and sea voyages.

He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.The depiction of folklore and myths is another genre in Huang’s wide repertoire and familiar subjects such as the Gods of Longevity, Fortune and Happiness are popular with him. While the three gods are typically represented respectfully in a line, standing equidistance from one another, Huang, however, has composed them in an atypical manner: crowded close to one other in a tight arrangement and utilizing the whole painting surface.

A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.“Wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings), derived from the archaic scripts of Chinese calligraphy, are Huang’s scholarly contributions to the artistic and literary fields. They are the fruit of his research for over a decade, and display his inventiveness, especially from the 1970s. Reflecting the title of this work, Huang illustrates a hermit with a calm disposition, in fluid stylized lines.

Here is Huai Su (737-799), a calligrapher best known for his wild cursive script, alone in a banana forest, bent over a stone as he writes. Huang appropriately creates an ambience of quiescent and Zen for Huai Su was also a monk.

Here, the benevolent looking Laughing Buddha is accompanied by two children balancing a bundle between them. The inscription aptly proclaims, “All’s well and delighted.”

Depicted here is the tale of how the warrior Zhou Chu, in a bid to redeem himself, killed the three threats to his village. With subtle tonal ink variations and different brushworks Huang captures the dramatic moment when Zhou Chu kills the dragon as it thrashes in the swirling waters.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Untitled (Visit the Elders with Two Children)
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Another genre in Huang’s wide repertoire is the painting of folklore and mythology. Tao Yuan Ming (365-427), a poet from the Southern Song dynasty had written a piece lamenting his mistake of joining officialdom; Huang depicts the happy scene of Tao’s return to his hometown, being welcomed by children and servants. An array of brushstrokes and linework is seen in this work, bearing testimony to Huang’s strong foundation in traditional ink painting.

In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang sought inspiration for his art from Chinese legends, folklore, mythology, history, literature and philosophy. The inscription that accompanies the painting of a man reclining in his chair, states, “After a meal, a nap and two brews of tea, I lift my head, only to realize the sun has set.” This quiet contentment with life is a theme that is seen in many of Huang’s works.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Awaiting Someone to Return from the Sea
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.In the later years, Huang experimented with other stylistic practices and thematic oeuvre that differed considerably from those of his earlier cartoon works. Travels around Southeast Asia brought new inspiration that resulted in paintings reflecting local subject matter such as this typical coastal scene. The title of the work is inscribed along the bottom edge of the painting.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.A marked difference in style and content from Huang’s earlier cartoon works can be seen in a series of works depicting scenes and scenery of Southeast Asia, which reflects local subject matter and elements. Malaysia, being one of the top exporters of rubber since the early 20th century, rubber plantations are a common sight in the country. In “Rubber Tapper” Huang depicts a plantation worker with her young child beside her. Like many paintings by him, Huang’s characters are innocent and cheerful.

The delicate lines and wet brushwork reveal Huang’s masterly control of the brush.

He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.In yet another painting reflecting life by the sea, Huang captures a family pulling in the net, harvesting the catch together. This work is part of a series of paintings that serves as a visual collection of Huang’s travels in the Southeast Asian region, which features the simple and rustic lives of villagers. The compositional device of framing with two coconut trees recalls a window opening through which the viewer quietly observes a scene from an idyllic world.

In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.After retirement, Huang was able to devote more time to art and travelled widely to seek subjects to paint. A collection of works, known as the “Nanyang series” features many paintings of Southeast Asian life; among which is “Welcoming Home”, a picture of simple joy whereby a child eagerly awaits as his mother and a younger sibling approach their stilt house (this type of house is common in rural areas in Southeast Asia).

Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s mastery of the art of traditional Chinese ink painting is evident through the complex and varied brushwork applied here. The hanging scroll format, which draws the viewer’s gaze to wander up the painting, is a device used in Chinese art. By entitling this work where a woman sits contentedly in her wooden hut “Divine Place” clearly shows Huang’s enchantment with the rural life of Southeast Asia.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Tang Poem : "Ascending the Stork Tower" by Wang Zhihuan
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang’s calligraphic paintings are often inspired by Tang and Song poems and idioms. This well-known poem, “Ascending the Stork Tower” is by Wang Zhihuan (688-742), a Tang poet. In the SAM collection is another illustration of the same poem by Huang. The unique feature of Huang’s calligraphic painting in which the painting plays a supporting role to the calligraphy-dominated work, is evident here.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang often created his “wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) based on poems and idioms, and used pictorial signs, usually from archaic scripts, as a source of inspiration. In this painting Huang deconstructed the archaic pictographs of the four characters of the idiom “hold up one’s head high”, before reassembling them.

As the word “abundance” is homophonically similar to the one for “fish” in Chinese, it is often symbolized by the fish motif. Therefore, a large fish is depicted to signify “Great Abundance”.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Self Reflection Three Times a Day
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.“Wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) are Huang’s scholarly contribution to the artistic and literary fields and are testament of his creativity. Derived from archaic scripts and pictorial forms, one of the characteristics of calligraphic painting is the emphasis on the Chinese characters while the pictorial elements are relegated to the background.

Born to a literati family in Shanghai, China, Huang Yao (1914-1987) received his formative education from his father in classical literati traditions and calligraphy, particularly in the archaic scripts of oracle bone and bronze inscription. In the 1930s, he gained recognition as a talented cartoonist for creating the comic character “Niu Bi Zi” which was widely used as an educational resource in schools. Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.Huang is best known for his unique creation of “wenzi hua” (calligraphic painting) which is the amalgamation of traditional Chinese culture and calligraphy, expressed in often seemingly abstract form. He took great delight in studying the ancient forms of the characters before deconstructing the different elements and recombining them in a new manner.

Huang travelled to Southeast Asia after the end of World War II in 1945, and resided in different countries before settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. He worked primarily as an art educator until his retirement in 1973. A prolific artist, he had held 23 solo and group exhibitions and six retrospective exhibitions.“Wenzi hua” (calligraphic paintings) are Huang’s scholarly contributions to the artistic and literary fields and they display his inventiveness, especially from the 1970s. After careful study Huang would replace characters with images or phonetically similar words. To illustrate “Quietness” (“an jing” in Chinese), the character “an” is represented by a woman under a shelter (in an identical composition with the Chinese character) and “jing” by a well as it has the same phonetic sound.

Possibly written in “zhongding wen” (bronze inscription), the title of a popular folklore covers the entire surface and an element of design is achieved through the use of elegant black lines.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Tang Poem : 3rd Line of "On Parting with the Buddhist Pilgrim Ling Che" by Liu Changqing
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Huang Yao was born to a literati family in 1914 in Shanghai. Trained by his father, Huang was familiar with a range of calligraphic styles. Huang resided in Vietnam, Hong Kong, Thailand before finally settling permanently in Malaysia in 1956. A total of 23 solo and group exhibitions and 6 retrospective exhibitions have been held in his name. He also drew comics under the pseudonym of Niu Bi Zi. Wenzi hua (Calligraphic Paintings) is a significant aspect of Huang’s scholarly contributions to the artistic and literary fields.Huang is known for his unique creation of “wenzi hua” (calligraphic painting) which is the amalgamation of traditional Chinese culture and calligraphy, expressed in often seemingly abstract form. He took great delight in studying the ancient forms of the characters before deconstructing and recombining them. The poem by Tang poet Liu Changqing (c. 709-786) is written on four separate papers, each illustrating a line from it.

Born in Ho Chi Minh City, Tran Trung Tin (b.1933) is a self-taught artist who began painting in 1969. He became a full-time artist in 1975 after the fall of Ho Chi Minh City, and moved in a circle of artists that included Bui Xuan Phai. He has had a solo exhibition in Vietnam, and has participated in group shows on Vietnamese art in the Netherlands, Singapore, Japan and the United States. The 1970s was Tin’s most prolific period and when impoverished for materials, he would work on newspaper, which served as a diary of Tin and the world.Tin’s works are largely a reflection of his feelings towards the war and its impact on the people. To him, the woman and mother figure symbolize strength; painted here is a woman, confidently stretched back, relaxing. This work would have been unacceptable then in Vietnam as Social Realism was the officially sanctioned art style.

The 1970s was Tin’s most prolific period and when impoverished for materials, he would work on newspaper, which served as a diary of Tin and the world.Tin’s works are largely expressions of his feelings. Painting his cat Meo was a catharsis and it exemplifies one of Tin’s motives for painting.

Born in China, Chen Wen Hsi settled in Singapore in 1949 and taught at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in the 1950s. Chen was instrumental in the development of the Nanyang Style and is recognised as one of the pioneer artists of Singapore, together with Chen Chong Swee, Georgette Chen, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang. Highly influential, Chen Wen Hsi is regarded as the doyen of Singapore Chinese-brush artists. Although he painted mainly in Chinese brush, he often ventured into a cubistic style, executed in either oil or ink. An ardent student of nature, Chen is best known for his paintings of gibbons and animals developed from the Lingnan style. 'Abstract Cranes' is composed entirely of planes of flat colour of various shapes, arranged against a blue background. Triangular shapes resemble paper folded cranes whilst the sharp corners recall beaks and wings.

Highly influential, Chen Wen Hsi is regarded as the doyen of Singapore Chinese-brush artists. Although he painted mainly in Chinese brush, he often ventured into a cubistic style, executed in either oil or ink. An ardent student of nature, Chen is best known for his paintings of gibbons and animals developed from the Lingnan style. In 'Two Figures', Chen’s use of exaggerated colours brings forth the intensity of tropical heat and light. A sense of immediacy is conveyed by its cropped composition. Chen has integrated his interests in Cubism and Expressionism with confidence and flair. Breaking the figures into angular facets accentuates their ruggedness and the rough-and-ready nature of their lifestyle. Coupled with Chen’s expressionistic brushwork, the canvas is filled with movement as light bounces off the myriad surfaces

Born in China, Chen Wen Hsi settled in Singapore in 1949 and taught at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in the 1950s. Chen was instrumental in the development of the Nanyang Style and is recognised as one of the pioneer artists of Singapore, together with Chen Chong Swee, Georgette Chen, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang. Highly influential, Chen Wen Hsi is regarded as the doyen of Singapore Chinese-brush artists. Although he painted mainly in Chinese brush, he often ventured into a cubistic style, executed in either oil or ink. An ardent student of nature, Chen is best known for his paintings of gibbons and animals developed from the Lingnan style. Chen is known to have created a series of more abstract ink paintings, which make greater use of washes and colour fields to suggest landscapes. 'The Mountain is Green', executed on gold paper, is a good example of this manner of painting. This painting is done in the traditional painterly mode, with emphasis on building up form through layer upon layer of texture strokes (cun fa).

Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.A multi-faceted artist, Cheong was one of the most experimental artists in the early art scene of Singapore. In the 1960s, Cheong explored new effects with oil and this painting is an example.

Sharifah Fatimah Zubir was born in Alor Star, Kedah in 1948. She received a Diploma in Fine Art from MARA before proceeding to obtain an MFA from Pratt Institute, New York. Zubir works in an abstract expressionist style of vibrant colours and energetic brushstrokes. Shapes are juxtaposed in dynamic compositions. In 2007, she became the first female artist in Malaysia to receive the title of Datuk.

Born in Fujian Province, Liu Kang (1911-2004) received his formal training in Shanghai Academy of Fine Art, where he was exposed to Chinese paintings. In 1928, he went to Paris where he was influenced by art movements such as Fauvism and Post-Impressionism. Not only was he one of Singapore’s pioneer artists, he was also a leading figure in the Society of Chinese Artists and the Singapore Art Society. In 1970, he was awarded the Public Service Star for his contributions in the field of art.In 1952, Liu, together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Cheong Soo Pieng, made the historic painting trip to Bali. Liu recorded the new sights and experiences through drawings and sketches; here is one of a Balinese drummer clad in traditional costume.

Born in Fujian Province, Liu Kang (1911-2004) received his formal training in Shanghai Academy of Fine Art, where he was exposed to Chinese paintings. In 1928, he went to Paris where he was influenced by art movements such as Fauvism and Post-Impressionism. Not only was he one of Singapore’s pioneer artists, he was also a leading figure in the Society of Chinese Artists and the Singapore Art Society. In 1970, he was awarded the Public Service Star for his contributions in the field of art.Mainly an oil painter, Liu started using pastel during the Japanese Occupation (1942-1945) as oil paints were unavailable then. He was subsequently attracted by the luminosity of pastel and preferred it to oil for his later depictions of the human figure.
Collection of National Gallery Singapore. © Estate of Cesar Torrente Legaspi

Born in China, Chen Wen Hsi settled in Singapore in 1949 and taught at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in the 1950s. Chen was instrumental in the development of the Nanyang Style and is recognised as one of the pioneer artists of Singapore, together with Chen Chong Swee, Georgette Chen, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang. Highly influential, Chen Wen Hsi is regarded as the doyen of Singapore Chinese-brush artists. Although he painted mainly in Chinese brush, he often ventured into a cubistic style, executed in either oil or ink. An ardent student of nature, Chen is best known for his paintings of gibbons and animals developed from the Lingnan style. 'Malayan Scenery' is an example where Chen has made full use of the dramatic power of the brush in Chinese painting, to depict a distinctly local setting. Although Chen adopts a Western fixed-point perspective in this work, the traditional vertical format gives full play to the fishing nets hung out to dry.

Born in China, Chen Wen Hsi settled in Singapore in 1949 and taught at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in the 1950s. Chen was instrumental in the development of the Nanyang Style and is recognised as one of the pioneer artists of Singapore, together with Chen Chong Swee, Georgette Chen, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang. Highly influential, Chen Wen Hsi is regarded as the doyen of Singapore Chinese-brush artists. Although he painted mainly in Chinese brush, he often ventured into a cubistic style, executed in either oil or ink. An ardent student of nature, Chen is best known for his paintings of gibbons and animals developed from the Lingnan style. Although undated, it is likely that this work was completed in the late 1940s to early 1950s, when Chen first settled in Singapore. The interest in everyday subjects and its style of painting and its style of painting is consistent with works found in his 1949 exhibition, Paintings by Mr Chen Wen Hsi.

Born in 1923 (d. 2002) in Ireland, Brother Joseph McNally arrived in Malaya in 1946 as a teacher with the De La Salle Order of Brothers. An accomplished painter and sculptor, Brother Joseph received in 1998 the Montblanc de la Culture Award. In 2004, his ‘Counsellor II’ was erected on the grounds of the National Museum of Ireland, County Mayo.The fairly clean outline of ‘Horseman, pass by’ resembles Brother Joseph’s 1968 painting ‘The Battle of Moytura’ – both inspired by Yeats. Here, rider and horse depend on each other for centre of gravity, the rearing posture of the individual and his waving hand, together with the subtle twist of the open-mouthed horse injects movement called to a halt in the work. The smooth knot of the head and the angular qualities found in the work are characteristic elements of balance and geometry that Brother commonly used in this period.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Wayang Puppets - Raja Batak and His Wife Boru Anian
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born in 1960 in Jakarta, Heri Dono studied at the Institut Seni Indonesia but dropped out of school to study wayang kulit (shadow puppet). He uses the traditional art form wayang kulit (shadow puppet) and adapts it as his contemporary medium of expression in his art. In addition, the influence this traditional art form can also be seen in Heri’s installations, performance arts as well as his paintings.Here, Heri conceives his own epic tale wayang legenda and creates his own characters using cardboards instead of leather like the traditional puppets. These puppets are characters from the wayang legenda that was first performed in 1988. The employment of humour is central to his work following the Indonesian dance and theatre tradition. Through the use of humour, clown characters or jokers are important characters for criticising and making fun of high society and are central to the drama. In this manner, Heri Dono’s plays as in his paintings and installations are social statements that he conveyed through the use of humour.

Born in 1941 in Malaysia, Tay Chee Toh studied at Singapore’s Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts and has a postgraduate degree (Oil Painting) from China. He is the recipient of numerous painting and sculpture awards, and in 1985 Tay was conferred the Government of Singapore’s Cultural Medallion Award.Created the year following ‘Indian Ladies’, ‘Red Aqua Magnum 1003’ displays a marked abstract shift in Tay’s style and imagery. In this batik work the bold colours explode onto the cloth, the fantastical non-figurative forms floating and flying through liquid space. They reflect Tay’s fascination at the time with amoebic and aquatic shapes and forms, inspiring him to create his ‘Aquatic series’ on batik. Of these abstract aquatic works, Tay has said they allowed him the freedom to move on, to evolve. It would mark the turning point in his artistic career.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Boats are one of Lim’s favourite subjects, and he has produced countless paintings of them. This is a rare work as instead of a group of boats in a busy port, it depicts a boat, secured with a thin rope, in isolation with little background.

Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.

He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.

In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003. Boats and ships are one of Lim’s favourite subjects and he has painted them throughout his artistic career. Although he began painting in the oil medium only in the early 1950s, this painting of a coolie boarding a Chinese junk shows his confident handling.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.This semi-abstract painting of Lake Toba and its surrounding hills is an early work by Lim in oil, a medium he started using in the 1950s before turning to focus on ink in the 1970s.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim started painting in oil in 1950s, focusing on ink painting only in 1970s. Tree is one of Lim’s favourite subjects, often taking the centre-stage, despite its location. With its sinewy trunks and thick branches, the tree here in the Balinese village is omnipresent, overshadowing the other architecture.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Painted in oil, a medium Lim first started using in the 1950s, this work depicts one of his favourite subjects – bumboats on the Singapore River. As Lim’s artworks are largely representational, this semi-abstract work is an attempt at experimentation into other art styles.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim started learning calligraphy from a young age, developing a strong foundation in Chinese calligraphy and mastering the art of the brush which he believed to be the fundamentals of Chinese ink painting. Writing in the wild cursive script (‘kuang cao’), this large work is a testament of Lim’s mastery in the art of calligraphy. While his earlier calligraphic works are refined and scholarly, his later ones are bold and expressive, full of life and vigour.

He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim started learning calligraphy from a young age, developing a strong foundation in Chinese calligraphy and mastering the art of the brush which he believed to be the fundamentals of Chinese ink painting. Writing in the wild cursive script, this bold and expressive work is a testament of Lim’s mastery in the art of calligraphy.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Believing that a good foundation in calligraphy is essential to the mastery of Chinese brush painting, Lim spent decades perfecting the art of calligraphy, assiduously studying ancient Chinese calligraphic masterpieces. His later calligraphic works, especially those created between 2000 and 2002 are distinctively bold, imposing and confident.

In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.An avid traveller, Lim often seeks out new subject matter for his art in the places he visits, returning home armed with new paintings. In Cambodia, the serene and enigmatic smiles of the famous landmark are captured in ‘Angkor Wat’.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim’s fascination with trees has resulted in numerous paintings of them. However around the turn of the century, his trees began to grow increasingly massive and entangled, often dominating the compositions. In ‘Kampong Scene’ the encroaching forest of tight branches, trunks and roots threatens to overwhelm the village huts.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim’s lifelong fascination with trees has resulted in numerous paintings of them which in recent years have become increasingly massive and entangled. In ‘Fishermen by the Sea’ fishermen mend their nets under the canopy of tight, interweaving branches.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.Lim has a lifelong fascination with trees and he produced countless paintings of them throughout his career. ‘Forest’ is a bold abstraction of interweaving and rhythmic lines that suggest branches, trunks and roots of the woods.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim, who has painted numerous trees throughout his career, has in recent years, allowed the trees to dominate as the main subject, grown increasingly massive. In ‘Pathway through the Forest’ a woman walks through an opening in the tightly entangled trees.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim’s fascination with trees is evident throughout his career and he has produced many paintings of them. In recent years, the trees have begun to dominate as the main subject, growing increasingly massive, overwhelming all other elements. In the village scene shown here, the tree, drawn with expressive and energetic brushwork, provides shade for a group of women under its spreading branches.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.A tireless traveller, Lim used to make painting trips around the region, at times alone, at times with artists such as the Ten Men Art Group which was active during the 1970s. As Lim has always been fascinated by the sea and boats it is not surprising that fishing villages feature often in his paintings.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Fascinated by boats and ships, Lim frequently features them in his works, in different settings, be it singly or in groups. Lim demonstrates his keen observation through the fine brushwork describing the boats by the shore. Well-controlled ink washes suffuse the sky and water surfaces, creating atmospheric effects.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.The bustling streets and charming back alleys of old Singapore have been a source of inspiration to Lim, and he is known to paint the same spots more than once. Yet, his paintings have remained fresh as he never merely depicts the physical elements, but the ever-changing atmosphere, life and activities of the people and the environment. The free and fluid brushstrokes display his confident handling and mastery of the ink medium.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.A lover of boats, Lim painted countless works of them in different settings: on the river, in the dock, undergoing repair or simply clustered together. In this painting, Lim demonstrates his keen observation through the fine brushwork describing the docked boat. His dexterity in the medium is seen in the light ink washes and the subtle tonal changes.

Following in the tradition of Chinese painting, Lim leaves vast surfaces unpainted, allowing the viewer to reflect on the composition and balance of the positive and negative spaces. This remarkable arrangement of flat planes and diagonals bring life to the otherwise abstracted space.

These women hail from the San Shui (three rivers) county of Guangdong and have arrived in Malaya since the early 19th century to seek a new life, often working as labourers in the construction and mining industries.

The free and fluid brushstrokes display his confident handling and mastery of the ink medium.

Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Having grown up in a ‘kampong’ (village in Malay), the idyllic life of rural communities is a favourite and recurring theme for Lim. In a bid to recapture the early memories, he has produced countless paintings depicting the life and activities of villagers. Although largely monochromatic, subtle tonal differences create depth in this landscape painting of a typical Malay village.

Fine and simple lines narrate the activity while the delicate ink washes suggest a sunny day.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Untitled (Women selling Sea Coconuts) 无题（贩卖海底椰的妇女）
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim pens in the inscription that accompanies the painting that this is a study of two coconut vendors whom he encountered in West Malaysia during a painting trip with a friend. A tireless traveller, Lim used to visit the neighbouring countries to paint, stopping only recently due to advancing age.

He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.Lines are paramount in Lim’s works, often taking precedence over other formal aspects of art-making, such as colour, space, texture, mass and volume. Here, complex linework makes up the sinewy tree trunk, stone sculptures and house in the background.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.A frequent traveller, Lim often went on painting trips to neighbouring countries to seek inspiration for his art which would tell the cultural and social narratives of a locale. Depicted here in his characteristic direct and representational style, through deft brushwork and ink washes, is a souvenir store selling masks.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Lim who prefers to paint on the spot to soak in the atmosphere of a place has captured in his paintings beyond just what the eye sees; his paintings often evoke sentiments of nostalgia in his viewers. This painting of a kitchen helper preparing roast ducks in a back alley, underneath a canopy hung from a sturdy tree, harks back to a simpler time.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.The bustling streets and charming back alleys of old Singapore are recurring subjects in Lim’s paintings. Despite depicting them countless times, he has managed to keep each work fresh as he never merely depicts the physical elements, but the changing atmosphere, life and activities of the people and the environment. The free and fluid brushstrokes display his confident handling and mastery of the ink medium.

He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.The streets of Singapore provided Lim with endless subject matter for his art. Often painting on site, his keen eye for detail and masterful handling of the ink medium have produced many works depicting life on the streets. However as urban renewal in the 1980s changed the cityscape, paintings such as this of back alley stalls, are invaluable reminders of a bygone era.
