verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: The Ocean Tide, Homage to Hokusai
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born in 1943 in Indonesia, Tan migrated to Singapore as a child. After graduating from the Nanyang University in Singapore in 1968, he worked at the French Embassy for 24 years before becoming a full-time artist. In 1985, Tan was awarded the Gold Medal of the French Artist Salon and in 1987, the Singapore Cultural Medallion. In 2003, Tan was honoured with the World Economic Forum Crystal Award and Singapore Meritorious Award. In 1993, Tan became the first Singapore artist to have a private museum dedicated to the display of his extensive oeuvre of artistic and literary works. This work is a tribute to 18th century Japanese artist-printmaker, Katsushika Hokusai, who is known for his iconic print ‘The Great Wave off Kanagawa’. An admirer of Hokusai, Tan feels the artist is the first to be able to depict a large wave beautifully. To create the feel and movement of waves, Tan experimented with different materials before he found success with acrylic. An innovative artist, Tan threw the paint in a controlled manner and splashed water droplets to achieve the effect of curls and foam.

Born in 1954 in Singapore, Ng Yak Hwee is a graduate of Singapore’s former Nanyang University (Botany major). An accomplished painter and sculptor, Ng received the 1987 Silver Medal from the Salon des Artistes Français, and won First Prize (Figurative category) in the 1992 UOB Painting of the Year Competition.Executed after the exhibition but stylistically and thematically similar to the paintings of Goh’s third solo show “Being with Nature”, ‘Autumn Mist’ and ‘Poem's Rock’ depict Goh’s familiar expressive bold strokes, abstract forms and subtly layered colours used to represent (real and imagined) scenes from nature. These works cap Goh’s evolving style during a decade of voluminous output and artistic development, in which the artist became primarily known as a landscape painter, but one who had gradually shifted into producing abstract paintings without any representational references by the late 1980s.

Born in Guangdong Province, China, Chen Chong Swee (1910-1985) came to Singapore in 1931 shortly after graduating from Xinhua Academy of Art, Shanghai. He co-founded the Salon Art Society (now the Singapore Society of Chinese Artists) in 1935 and was a teacher with Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts for over 20 years. In 1952, Chen, together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang, went on what would eventually turn out be a historically significant trip to Bali, Indonesia in search of new inspiration and subject matter. As one of the first artists instrumental in developing the Nanyang Style, Chen pioneered attempts to interpret local landscapes according to the Chinese concept of pictorial composition with Western watercolour techniques.Chen’s works are often inspired by his surroundings and local subject matter, such as this scene which is a common sight along the coasts of Southeast Asia. The composition is divided almost exactly in half, between the sky and ground, and the painting captures a sunny day as the fishermen go about their business of drying nets.

Born in Guangdong Province, China, Chen Chong Swee (1910-1985) came to Singapore in 1931 shortly after graduating from Xinhua Academy of Art, Shanghai. He co-founded the Salon Art Society (now the Singapore Society of Chinese Artists) in 1935 and was a teacher with Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts for over 20 years. In 1952, Chen, together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang, went on what would eventually turn out be a historically significant trip to Bali, Indonesia in search of new inspiration and subject matter. As one of the first artists instrumental in developing the Nanyang Style, Chen pioneered attempts to interpret local landscapes according to the Chinese concept of pictorial composition with Western watercolour techniques.Throughout Chen’s artistic career, his style remained realistic as he believed that the objective of a painting must be understood by its viewer; his works are thus often inspired by his surroundings. ‘Flutist’ was painted in Bali in 1953.

Born in Guangdong Province, China, Chen Chong Swee (1910-1985) came to Singapore in 1931 shortly after graduating from Xinhua Academy of Art, Shanghai. He co-founded the Salon Art Society (now the Singapore Society of Chinese Artists) in 1935 and was a teacher with Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts for over 20 years. In 1952, Chen, together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang, went on what would eventually turn out be a historically significant trip to Bali, Indonesia in search of new inspiration and subject matter. As one of the first artists instrumental in developing the Nanyang Style, Chen pioneered attempts to interpret local landscapes according to the Chinese concept of pictorial composition with Western watercolour techniques.

Born in Guangdong Province, China, Chen Chong Swee (1910-1985) came to Singapore in 1931 shortly after graduating from Xinhua Academy of Art, Shanghai. He co-founded the Salon Art Society (now the Singapore Society of Chinese Artists) in 1935 and was a teacher with Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts for over 20 years. In 1952, Chen, together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Cheong Soo Pieng and Liu Kang, went on what would eventually turn out be a historically significant trip to Bali, Indonesia in search of new inspiration and subject matter. As one of the first artists instrumental in developing the Nanyang Style, Chen pioneered attempts to interpret local landscapes according to the Chinese concept of pictorial composition with Western watercolour techniques.Besides painting in watercolour and Chinese ink, Chen also painted in oil. His artistic style however remained realistic as he believed that the objective of a painting must be understood by its viewer. ‘Balinese Women’, painted in the year of the seminal trip to Bali, depicts two women with baskets of fruits. The rich cultural life and natural beauty of the tropical island continued to fascinate Chen long after his first trip there in 1952.

Vincent Leow (1961) has carved out an important role in Singapore's contemporary art development. He works in a wide range of genres, from performances, installations and sculptures, to digital and mixed-media works. One of the early members of The Artists Village, founded in 1988, Leow's art has been about emphasizing the 'process' in art where he constantly challenges how people consume and understand art; either through playful or antagonistic means. Leow's role as an educator has also inspired today's generation of young contemporary artists who are interested in works of a similar vein.'Fu Lu Shuo' could be regarded as a study as well as precedence to Vincent''s oeuvre representing fictionalized narratives about the composite of different cultures. This etching which was produced in 1987, while Leow was pursuing his Diploma at LASALLE College of the Arts, may well be a significant step towards his venture in interrogating contemporary issues, interweaving them with myths and fables.

Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972. In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. Since the 1980s, Chng has been instrumental in promoting interest in printmaking and has held more than 20 solo exhibitions. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.Chng’s works are often inspired by nature and water, one of her favourite motifs, has been studied and depicted extensively. ‘Flowing Series’ showcases four prints of water in different light, mood and agitation.

Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972. In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. Since the 1980s, Chng has been instrumental in promoting interest in printmaking and has held more than 20 solo exhibitions. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.In the early 1980s, Chng became interested in the I-Ching (Book of Changes)—an ancient Chinese text that centers on the law of Yin and Yang, the idea of evolution of events and the acceptance of change—as a resource. ‘I Ching Modulation’ is a result of her investigations into variation and chance.

Born in 1963, Chng Chin Kang graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA) in 1988, and received his master’s degree from University of Central England in Birmingham in 1996. Since his return from England, there has been a departure in style as Chng veers towards realist painting to depict his observations about Singapore society at large. He is best known for painting on printed linen, often using bedsheets instead of canvas. In 1998, Chng’s painting won the prestigious UOB Painting of the Year. Chng has since participated in many exhibitions in Singapore, Thailand and Japan. In this work, painted probably during or immediately after Chng’s graduation from NAFA, the interplay of hues and tones, and the two figures dancing in exultance emit a feeling of happiness and freedom, exemplifying the title, ‘Let it be’.

Tang Da Wu, born in 1943, is widely regarded as the central figure in the development of ‘alternative’ art in Singapore. A graduate from Goldsmith College, University of London with a Master of Art, he led a group of younger artists to establish The Artists Village in Singapore in 1988, where performance, installation and painting took place. Since the late 1990s, Tang has been working on community projects that deal with memory, history as well as environmental issues. In 1999, Tang was awarded the Arts and Culture Prize at the 10th Fukuoka Asian Culture Prizes and in 2007, he was one of four artists who represented Singapore at the Venice Biennale.Tang’s works are often thoughts-invoking, allowing the viewer to interpret his works in their own way. In the center of The Africano, Tang painted a creature that has a vulture-like head and a human like form, seemingly leaning against a yellow wall.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Fire Engine, Rice Bowl and Flag
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Tang Da Wu, born in 1943, is widely regarded as the central figure in the development of ‘alternative’ art in Singapore. Since the late 1990s, Tang has been working on community projects that deal with memory, history as well as environmental issues. In 1999, Tang was awarded the Arts and Culture Prize at the 10th Fukuoka Asian Culture Prizes and in 2007, he was one of four artists who represented Singapore at the Venice Biennale.In this semi-representational ink work, wisps of line suggest slight movement of water around the woman and fish.

Born in 1954 in Singapore, Ng Yak Hwee is a graduate of Singapore’s former Nanyang University (Botany major). An accomplished painter and sculptor, Ng received the 1987 Silver Medal from the Salon des Artistes Français, and won First Prize (Figurative category) in the 1992 UOB Painting of the Year Competition.As a self-taught artist, Goh has experimented with various styles since the 1970s and explored the use of a wide range of medium in the process, such as batik painting, paper collage, acrylic and oil painting, and found object sculpture. In this collage on canvas Goh has immersed himself fully into the abstract realm and unlike his other landscape works from the same period he has not included any literal references. The work presents an aerial view through the clouds to the patchwork landscape with roads lying below, as if viewed from the window of an overhead-flying airplane.

Born in China in 1934, Lee Boon Wang is one of Singapore’s foremost oil painters. After graduating from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1953, Lee became a full-time artist in 1973. In 1982, Lee was awarded the Singapore Distinction Art Award and in 1989, he was the first Singaporean commissioned to paint the Royal Family of Brunei. Up till 2003, Lee has held seven solo exhibitions, and has participated in seven other group exhibitions in Singapore, New York, Moscow and Brunei.The Singapore River is a source of inspiration for Lee and he has produced many works of this busy waterway that is lined with shophouses and godowns. This painting was completed near the end of the decade-long clean-up campaign started by the Singapore government in 1977.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.From 1981 to 1983, Lim raced against time to depict the streets scenes of Singapore before they were transformed by redevelopment. In this work, Lim paints the bustling roadside activity outside a cakeshop during a festive season, as stated in the inscription.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Reflecting his background as a calligrapher, lines rather than tones are paramount in Lim’s art. In this painting of Bali, delicate linework is used in the depiction of the magnificent tree, its spreading branches and the ubiquitous shrines that dot the island landscape.

Born in Singapore in 1946, Nai Swee Leng who graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1966 was one of the founders of the Molan Art Association and a committee member of the Society of Chinese Artists until 1992. He is actively engaged in the local and international art scenes, participating in solo and joint exhibitions; his first solo exhibition was held in 1974.Nai first learnt about the Shanghai School of painting when he studied under Fan Chang Tien; and the Lingnan School, another branch of Chinese ink painting, from Chao Shao An. Besides painting birds and flowers, a genre in traditional ink painting that forms a large part of Nai’s repertoire, he also paints local fruits and plants.

Born in Singapore in 1964, Jimmy Ong won the 1980 UOB Painting of the Year Competition (Youth 2nd Prize), and during the mid-1980s to early-1990s received scholarships to study in the USA and in Italy. Accomplished in sculpture and painting, Ong’s trademark are his expressive charcoal drawings.During Ong’s year at the Center for Creative Studies in Detroit (1984-85) he learnt the fundamentals of life drawing, while frequent visits to the Detroit Institute of Art sparked a particular interest in the (self-) portrait drawings by Michelangelo, Kokoschka, Nolde and other German Expressionists. Following his return to Singapore, Ong concentrated on self-portraits and still lifes before turning to his friends as subjects for his drawings. With these works he has attempted to capture for posterity not only a physical likeness, but also a certain emotion particular to that time and place; recording significant moments of his life.

Born in 1946 in Singapore, Chong Fah Cheong is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer sculptors. A teacher by training, Chong is a graduate from the University of Singapore, and has a Diploma in Curriculum Studies in Art and Design from the City of Birmingham Polytechnic, United Kingdom. He taught art in Singapore schools before becoming a professional artist in 1978. In 1989, Chong moved to Canada where he currently lives and works but makes regular trips back to Singapore to engage with the local art scene and participate in exhibitions.In 1979, the Singapore government started the Speak Mandarin campaign to urge Chinese Singaporeans to use Mandarin, one of the four official languages, instead of dialect. This work, inspired by the logo of the campaign, raises questions of what makes a mother tongue as the Chinese population is made up of many different dialect groups for which Mandarin is not a native language.

Born in 1946 in Singapore, Chong Fah Cheong is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer sculptors. A teacher by training, Chong is a graduate from the University of Singapore, and has a Diploma in Curriculum Studies in Art and Design from the City of Birmingham Polytechnic, United Kingdom. He taught art in Singapore schools before becoming a professional artist in 1978. In 1989, Chong moved to Canada where he currently lives and works but makes regular trips back to Singapore to engage with the local art scene and participate in exhibitions.Largely self-taught, Chong is a versatile sculptor whose works range from realistic to abstract forms. He began his sculpting career working with wood but has since diversified to include stone and metal. Chong’s artworks are often informed by his travels and experiences; ‘Mask’ is inspired by the protective mask used in Kendo, a traditional Japanese martial art.

Born in 1931, Choo Keng Kwang is widely recognized for realistic renditions of landscape, animals and nature in the oil medium. Sympathetic to local working classes, Choo features them in many of his works. Graduating in 1953 from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), he worked as a teacher, eventually returning to NAFA to helm the Art Education Department in 1984. Choo has since held many solo exhibitions and has participated in group art exhibitions in Southeast Asia, Japan, Europe and USA. Since the 1950s, Choo has received many awards for his many contributions to art and education, among which is the Public Service Medal (PBM) in 1976.Sungei Road is home of one of the oldest flea markets in Singapore. Popularly known as Thieves Market, street peddlers selling second-hand items line the lanes near Rochor Canal. Although the market had its heyday in the 1980s it is still frequented these days by bargain-hunters. This painting by Choo takes an unusual view of Sungei Road as it does not show the street peddlers but instead focuses on the architecture of a two-storey building on that street.

Born in Singapore in 1955, Lim Tiong Ghee is a self-taught artist renowned for his abstract collages. He was Category Winner (Representational), UOB Painting of the Year Competition in 1984 with his ‘Reflection’ watercolour painting, and in 1989 Lim received the Competition’s Top Award for his wrapping paper collage ‘From the Turtledove’.By the late 1980s, the still life had replaced the human figure as subject matter in Lim’s work. Although Lim has used watercolour on paper here, and the image still retains figurative elements, the picture plane is decisively segmented into freestanding shapes and blocks of colour, as if separately collaged onto the surface. It is an indication of Lim’s developmental shift into his signature collages, one of which will win an award in the same year this work was created, and as such this painting can be viewed as a possible preparatory study for a subsequent collage.

Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972. In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. Since the 1980s, Chng has been instrumental in promoting interest in printmaking and has held more than 20 solo exhibitions. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.

Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972.  In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. Since the 1980s, Chng has been instrumental in promoting interest in printmaking and has held more than 20 solo exhibitions. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.In ‘Cosmopolis’, Chng depicts a colourful cityscape of towering buildings with dark lines running across the work. On closer examination, one sees people filing orderly into and out of the buildings, underscoring the complex relationship of life in a modern city.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: The Last Tango on the Sea
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born in 1946, Chng Seok Tin graduated from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1972. In 1980, she took up post-graduate studies in printmaking which was followed by a year's practice under S.W. Hayter at Atelier 17, Paris. Since the 1980s, Chng has been instrumental in promoting interest in printmaking and has held more than 20 solo exhibitions. In May 2005, Chng was the first Singaporean to hold a solo exhibition at the United Nations Headquarter and was awarded the Cultural Medallion in the same year.As part of the development from Chng’s water print series, ‘The Last Tango on the Sea’, although dream-like, is a grim reminder that the actions of man may lead to the destruction of the earth.

Born in Fujian Province, Liu Kang (1911-2004) received his formal training in Shanghai Academy of Fine Art, where he was exposed to Chinese paintings. In 1928, he went to Paris where he was influenced by art movements such as Fauvism and Post-Impressionism. Not only was he one of Singapore’s pioneer artists, he was also a leading figure in the Society of Chinese Artists and the Singapore Art Society. In 1970, he was awarded the Public Service Star for his contributions in the field of art.This is an important work which provides deeper insights into Liu’s early years of practice. The work, completed during his Paris years, encapsulates a period when Liu was exposed to many masterpieces in Europe. ‘Farmer’s House’ is an example where Liu makes reference to Vincent van Gogh’s style, especially the Dutch artist’s distinctive brush strokes.

Born in Fujian Province, Liu Kang (1911-2004) received his formal training in Shanghai Academy of Fine Art, where he was exposed to Chinese paintings. In 1928, he went to Paris where he was influenced by art movements such as Fauvism and Post-Impressionism. Not only was he one of Singapore’s pioneer artists, he was also a leading figure in the Society of Chinese Artists and the Singapore Art Society. In 1970, he was awarded the Public Service Star for his contributions in the field of art.From 1928 to 1933, when Liu was in Paris, he was influenced by the major movements there, having access to the works of the masters in the museums. ‘Breakfast’ hints of Fauvist sensitivity in the choice of colour, form and design.

Born in 1939 in Singapore (d. 2005, Singapore), Tay Bak Koi graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960. Adept at painting in watercolour and oil, Tay developed a distinctive style of simplified forms and reduced lines. During 1961-1970 Tay completed 300 watercolours for the then Hilton Suites, Singapore.By the late 1980s, Tay had honed his unique recognisable style as a painter, and two paintings effortlessly testify to this: ‘Tranquility’ and ‘Country Scene - Grey’. A continuation of a familiar theme, both works feature an imaginary and remote landscape where a single dwelling resides in peaceful isolation. There are the usual sprinkling of details, contrasting strokes and the high vantage point, as if viewed through the clouds. Tay has executed ‘Tranquility’ in cool tones, while ‘Country Scene - Grey’ is in warm colours. When viewed together the similarities are prevented from being overly repetitive by Tay’s exploration of colour.

This work has been adopted by Lam Soon Cannery Pte Ltd Born in Singapore in 1959, Henri Chen Kezhan is a graduate from Baharuddin Vocational Institute (1979) and the Ecole des Beaux Arts, Paris (1986). He was also a student of eminent artists Fan Chang Tien (1907-1987) and Zhao Shao-ang (1905-1998). Since 1977, Chen has exhibited in solo and group shows locally and internationally, and in 2001 he was one of four artists who represented Singapore in the 49th Venice Biennale. In 1985, Chen was awarded the silver medal by the Salon des Artistes France and in 1993, the Young Artist Award by the Singapore government.In the series entitled Self-Portraits, Chen features the lotus which embodies, in Chinese culture, the values of purity and virtue.

One of the most prolific and talented artists in Singapore, Ng Eng Teng produced more than 300 sculptures over a span of 40 years. These included large-scale public sculptures to life-sized and smaller sculptures. His art education started in Singapore as a painting student at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), before continuing his studies in industrial pottery in North Staffordshire College of Technology, England. He went on to study studio pottery in Farnham School of Art and subsequently became a designer in a pottery factory in Ireland for a few years. These various stints equipped him with the necessary technical vocabulary to deal with the 3-dimensional form.From the Dancer series which dates back to around 1990, ‘Dancer’ continues Ng’s investigation into re-looking at the human torso. In this series, although the torso has been reduced and simplified, the figure is still the central preoccupation. However beyond examining formal qualities, there is an attempt by Ng to capture the essence of a dancer in motion.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: Ming Bowl and Three Coloured Teapot
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Born in 1941 in Malaysia, Tay Chee Toh studied at Singapore’s Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts and has a postgraduate degree (Oil Painting) from China. He is the recipient of numerous painting and sculpture awards, and in 1985 Tay was conferred the Government of Singapore’s Cultural Medallion Award.Dating from between 1988 and 1990, the Singapore Art Museum has several of Tay’s watercolour paintings of still lives and flowers. Created during a period of great artistic output and experimentation by the artist, it is almost curious to see Tay returning to realism with these delicate studies on paper. Like many artists of his generation who passed through the Academy, Tay’s observational and drawing skills are finely honed. These formal academic watercolours appear to present themselves as testimonials, as if the artist has temporarily removed himself from the frenzy of abstract activity to re-focus on the coordination of hand and eye.

Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.From 1961 to 1963, Cheong travelled around Europe to learn more about Western art and the contemporary art movements. ‘London Scene’ is painted during this two-year sojourn. It was also during this same period that Cheong, an accomplished Chinese ink painter, underwent a phase of experimentation with Chinese ink.

Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Cheong’s varied stylistic oeuvre is a testimony of his experimental spirit. ‘Sunset Landscape’ which attempts to create the atmospheric effects of sunset rather than representing a known landscape, is painted mainly in grey hues, suffused with orange in the horizon.

Born in 1932 in Hainan, China, Aw Tee Hong grew up in Malaysia before settling in Singapore. A graduate from Singapore’s Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1954 and the Beijing Central Academy of Arts & Design in 1958, Aw has held over 21 solo art exhibitions around the world since the early 1970s. A second generation Nanyang artist with a great passion for nature, Aw is conversant in oil painting, Chinese ink, watercolour, mural painting and sculpture.

Born in 1937 in Indonesia, Goh Beng Kwan migrated with his family to Singapore in 1945. In the 1950s, he studied Chinese ink painting with Chen Wen Hsi. In 1963, he studied at the Art Students' League in New York where he was first introduced to collage art and Abstract Expressionism. Goh was awarded the inaugural UOB Painting of the Year First Prize in 1982 and the Cultural Medallion in 1989.Goh is noted for his collage works with contain an “oriental perspective”. Inspired by nature, environment and his ethnic Chinese heritage, his works relate to sensory impressions of the places he has visited. Goh’s works are mainly mixed media pieces which use natural and man-made materials.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.The Singapore River, one of Lim’s favourite subjects, appears in his paintings time and again. Yet, the river under his brush is never the same as he challenges himself to depict its different charms each time.

Yet, the river under his brush is never the same as he challenges himself to depict its different charms each time.

Born in 1951, Teng Nee Cheong studied in Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (1966-1968) where he was taught by Georgette Chen and Ng Yat Chuan. It was here that he developed his lifelong interest in life drawing. In 1978, Teng was presented with the Ministry of Culture Special Award and in 1991, the Tan Tsze Chor Art Award. Teng had his first solo exhibition at the National Museum Art Gallery in 1980 and since then, he has exhibited widely, including in France, Brunei and Japan.Proficient in several mediums, including oil, pastel and charcoal, Teng is well regarded for his still life paintings, especially of flowers. In this pastel work of hibiscus, his signature style whereby the blooms are set off against a patterned fabric is employed.

Born in 1936, Thomas Yeo graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960. He then studied in Chelsea School of Art and Hammersmith College of Art and Architecture in London. Since then, Yeo has participated in more than 100 solo and group exhibitions and in 1984, he was awarded the Cultural Medallion. In Two Boats by the Stream, Yeo achieves balance with the trees on the banks on either side of the artwork. Yeo presents to the viewers an overall sense of tranquillity through the use of mainly cool colours. Yet not all is calm. This tranquillity is also disturbed as the viewer senses the strength of water gushing through the inlet and the slight movements of the two boats on the right side of the stream.

Born in 1936, Thomas Yeo graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960. He then studied in Chelsea School of Art and Hammersmith College of Art and Architecture in London. Since then, Yeo has participated in more than 100 solo and group exhibitions and in 1984, he was awarded the Cultural Medallion. Landscape and nature are predominant themes in Yeo’s oeuvre. In Dawn, Yeo brings us a view from the top of a hill. Roofs of houses can be seen just behind the trees depicted in the foreground. Yeo demarcates the background by creating a mist-like space in the middle-ground. The use of warm colours in the artwork presents an atmosphere of hope and freshness, like the start of a day.

Born in Singapore in 1955, Lim Tiong Ghee is a self-taught artist renowned for his abstract collages. He was Category Winner (Representational), UOB Painting of the Year Competition in 1984 with his ‘Reflection’ watercolour painting, and in 1989 Lim received the Competition’s Top Award for his wrapping paper collage ‘From the Turtledove’.In Lim’s earliest collages, figurative images of tropical flora, luscious fruits, and Chinese delicacies are predominant. As Lim honed his technical skills and style, he drew inspiration from his immediate surroundings, taking his references from everyday and readily available examples. There is a strong decorative element – a pleasing balance of composed shapes of paper and a harmonious layering of translucent colours, neatly contained and bordered within the base paper’s edges. The works are delicate without appearing fragile, with stylistic hints of Lim’s Chinese heritage, all characteristics mimicking the qualities of the paper used.

Born in Fuzhou, China, Yeh Chi Wei (1915-1981) is included among a group referred to as Singapore’s “first generation artists”. Graduating from Shanghai’s Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in 1936, Yeh worked as an art teacher throughout Malaya and Singapore until 1964, while an active member of various art groups. He was especially noted in having started a series of painting trips since 1960 to various Southeast Asian and Asian countries under the Ten Men Group, as well as the Ten Men Art Exhibitions after such trips.The five paintings, ‘Portrait of a Dyak Lady’, ‘The Dyak Plays the Musical Instrument’, ‘Drummer’, ‘Musician’ and ‘Untitled’ (Accession No. P-0752), are examples of Yeh’s experimentation with incorporating diverse Western and Eastern influences and interests into the treatment of his figurative subjects. His elongated figures are rendered in black, framed by textured fields of muted colour. Yeh’s quiet and thoughtful approach brought to each painting, distils the subject matter down to its essence both in terms of its narrative, composition and painterly technique. His depiction of indigenous cultures offers a world rich with evocative inner meaning.
Collection of National Gallery Singapore. © Family of Tay Bak Koi

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: S'pore River - From Raffles Landing Site
verified_roots_caption_records.caption: Ong Kim Seng, born in 1945 and one of Singapore’s best known watercolourists, is largely a self-taught artist who learnt by observing pioneer watercolourists Lim Cheng Hoe (1912-1979) and Gog Sing Hooi (1933-1994) who painted by the Singapore River on Sundays. Ong, whose first solo exhibition in 1979 featured works from his 1978 trek to the Himalayas, became a full-time artist in 1985. In 1999 Ong was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government, and in 2000 the prestigious Dolphin Fellowship by the American Watercolour Society, the only Asian to receive such an honour.

Born in 1943 in Indonesia, Tan migrated to Singapore as a child. After graduating from the Nanyang University in Singapore in 1968, he worked at the French Embassy for 24 years before becoming a full-time artist. In 1985, Tan was awarded the Gold Medal of the French Artist Salon and in 1987, the Singapore Cultural Medallion. In 2003, Tan was honoured with the World Economic Forum Crystal Award and Singapore Meritorious Award. In 1993, Tan became the first Singapore artist to have a private museum dedicated to the display of his extensive oeuvre of artistic and literary works. 'Night Kites’ represents an enchanting amalgamation of Tan’s various interests. Executed in traditional Chinese painting format, Tan completely covers the painting surface, a concept more akin to Western art. Drips and splotches of colour take shape as kites, resembling marine creatures floating across a watery universe – a universe that is reflective of the Buddhist tenet that all things change and take on different forms. Trained in Chinese calligraphy, Tan started on acrylic late in his artistic career and found the medium allows him to create strong dark contrasts and warmth on raw rice-paper.

Born in 1929 in Xiamen, China, Lim Yew Kuan migrated to Singapore during the Second World War. A graduate from the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA) in 1950 and a teacher there for the next seven years, Lim furthered his studies at the Chelsea School of Art in London till 1962. Returning to Singapore in 1963, he became Acting Principal of NAFA when his father Lim Hak Tai, NAFA’s founding principal passed away. In 1980, Lim became NAFA’s Vice Principal.

verified_roots_caption_records.rootsTitle: A Hot Day by the river

Teng had his first solo exhibition at the National Museum Art Gallery in 1980 and since then, he has exhibited widely, including in France, Brunei and Japan.

Goh was awarded the inaugural UOB Painting of the Year First Prize in 1982 and the Cultural Medallion in 1989.Goh is noted for his collage works with contain an “oriental perspective”. Inspired by nature, environment and his ethnic Chinese heritage, his works relate to sensory impressions of the places he has visited. Goh’s works are mainly mixed media pieces which use natural and man-made materials.

Born in 1946 in Singapore, Chong Fah Cheong is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer sculptors. A teacher by training, Chong is a graduate from the University of Singapore, and has a Diploma in Curriculum Studies in Art and Design from the City of Birmingham Polytechnic, United Kingdom. He taught art in Singapore schools before becoming a professional artist in 1978. In 1989, Chong moved to Canada where he currently lives and works but makes regular trips back to Singapore to engage with the local art scene and participate in exhibitions.While Chong is known for his sculptures that reflect and comment on societal issues, at times, the challenge of working in wood, the process of carving is in itself, to him the art. In creating ‘Shell’, which is hollowed out of one side with a fluid gentle rise on the reverse, he did not begin with a specific idea but to simply explore the potential of this piece of teak wood and to reveal the form within.
Collection of National Gallery Singapore. © Family of Tay Bak Koi

Born in 1939 in Singapore (d. 2005, Singapore), Tay Bak Koi graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960. Adept at painting in watercolour and oil, Tay developed a distinctive style of simplified forms and reduced lines. During 1961-1970 Tay completed 300 watercolours for the then Hilton Suites, Singapore.By the late 1980s, Tay had clearly established a unique recognisable style as a painter. He frequently painted works in either cool or monochromatic colours, in the process (especially when painting with oils) relying on textural surfaces to proved the required contrasting effect. He depicted a reality that was an amalgamation of imaginary and real, and which often had nature and peacefulness at its narrative core. And he sought to distil complex forms to their basic shapes that were emphasised by decorative details and finely tuned line work. All of these characteristics can be identified here in his ‘Fishing Rocks’.

Born in 1939 in Singapore (d. 2005, Singapore), Tay Bak Koi graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960. Adept at painting in watercolour and oil, Tay developed a distinctive style of simplified forms and reduced lines. During 1961-1970 Tay completed 300 watercolours for the then Hilton Suites, Singapore.As with his ‘Village on Stilts’, Tay has chosen the ‘kelongs’ – Malay for offshore wooden huts built on stilts by fishermen and connected to land via a wooden gangway – as the subject matter for this painting. Unlike the aforementioned work, however, ‘Water Village’ is a study in paired-down simplicity. His monochromatic colours seamlessly merge the tangible with the intangible; shape, form and line coalesce on a horizon-less plane. Tay offers the viewer not just a glimpse into a watery way of life near extinction, but also a lesson in restrained design and calculated composition.
Collection of National Gallery Singapore. © Family of Tay Bak Koi

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.The bustling street life of Singapore provided Lim with endless subject matter for his art. Often painting on site in Chinese ink as the medium allows him to paint with speed, he has produced many works that capture the energy and dynamism of the streets. Lim’s keen eye and careful linework detail a mind-boggling array of goods sold in ‘Sundry Shop’.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.The bustling street life of early Singapore provided Lim with endless subject matter for his art. Often painting on site in Chinese ink as the medium allows him to paint with speed, Lim’s keen eye for detail and masterful handling of the medium have produced works that capture the energy and dynamism of the streets. His paintings now serve as reminders of a bygone era as many of these sights have vanished due to urban development.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.The bustling street life of early Singapore provided Lim with endless subject matter for his art. Often painting on site in Chinese ink as the medium allows him to paint with speed, Lim has produced works that capture the energy and dynamism of the streets. As many of these sights have vanished due to urban development, his paintings now serve as reminders of a bygone era.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.The streets of early Singapore provided Lim with endless subject matter for his art. In ‘Keng Chew Street’, his eye for detail has captured the intricacies of living in shophouses, as well as street life. As urban development continues to take place in Singapore, this painting is a window to the past for the younger generation.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.Although Lim is known for his Chinese ink paintings, he is also a skilful calligraphic exponent who counts the expressive cursive script as his one of his favourite.

Tang Da Wu, born in 1943, is widely regarded as the central figure in the development of ‘alternative’ art in Singapore. A graduate from Goldsmith College, University of London with a Master of Art, he led a group of younger artists to establish The Artists Village in Singapore in 1988, where performance, installation and painting took place. Since the late 1990s, Tang has been working on community projects that deal with memory, history as well as environmental issues. In 1999, Tang was awarded the Arts and Culture Prize at the 10th Fukuoka Asian Culture Prizes and in 2007, he was one of four artists who represented Singapore at the Venice Biennale.

Tang Da Wu, born in 1943, is widely regarded as the central figure in the development of ‘alternative’ art in Singapore. A graduate from Goldsmith College, University of London with a Master of Art, he led a group of younger artists to establish The Artists Village in Singapore in 1988, where performance, installation and painting took place. Since the late 1990s, Tang has been working on community projects that deal with memory, history as well as environmental issues. In 1999, Tang was awarded the Arts and Culture Prize at the 10th Fukuoka Asian Culture Prizes and in 2007, he was one of four artists who represented Singapore at the Venice Biennale.‘Wolf in the Basement’ is an enigmatic work in which a man, lying on the ground while his companion sits nearby, peers over an edge to a wolf below. In this monochromatic work, texture and contrast are created by lines and tonal variations.

Born in 1946 in Malaysia, self-taught painter and sculptor Thang Kiang How’s talent and community service is variously recognised, including: Singapore’s Public Service Medal (1981), Dr Tan Tsze Chor Award (1982), and Special Award, UOB Painting of the Year competition (1985, 1986 and 1987). Exhibiting nationally and internationally, Thang was also President of the Modern Art Society Singapore (1981-93).Following his second solo exhibition in 1989, held at Singapore’s National Museum Art Gallery, Thang continued painting his “Galaxies’ series that then expanded into the “Second Life of Galaxies” series. As with his other ‘Galaxies’ works, this second series consists mostly of square canvasses or sheets of paper with acrylic and compressed air detailing. However, a significant compositional development in the series occurred following an incident with a destructive dog – a completed painting is cut into strips then reconfigured, for example as with ‘Galaxy 228’ and ‘Galaxy Series No. 231’.

One of the most prolific and talented artists in Singapore, Ng Eng Teng produced more than 300 sculptures over a span of 40 years. These included large-scale public sculptures to life-sized and smaller sculptures. His art education started in Singapore as a painting student at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), before continuing his studies in industrial pottery in North Staffordshire College of Technology, England. He went on to study studio pottery in Farnham School of Art and subsequently became a designer in a pottery factory in Ireland for a few years. These various stints equipped him with the necessary technical vocabulary to deal with the 3-dimensional form.‘Three in One’ is hollowed out to give it a particular ‘lightness’ and when viewed from the top, it resembles a fork. With each tine equally carved out from the circular base, it offers the viewer a sense of unity–a pervading motif in many of Ng’s works.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is largely a self-taught artist. In his oeuvre, ink paintings and calligraphic works feature prominently, although there is a small number of oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. Lim, a school principal until his retirement in the early 1980s, was awarded the Cultural Medallion by the Singapore government in 2003.When rapid urban redevelopment in the 1980s threatened to change the face of Singapore streets forever, Lim raced against time to record his favourite haunts on paper; in three years, Lim drew some 300 paintings. In the ‘Sundry Shop’ Lim’s eye for detail and his fine, intricate linework capture the many products that are displayed on the walkway and cramp the interior of the quaint shop.

Born in China in 1915 (d. 1996, Singapore), Hou Hsi Ching migrated to Singapore in 1948. A self-taught artist in traditional Chinese brush painting and calligraphy, Hou was awarded the Special Award, National Day Art Exhibition (1971) and the Dr Tan Tze Chor Art Award (1991).In a 1983 Singapore POST feature article, Hou was referred to as ‘The orchid painter’. In his never-ending quest to identify local subject matter, he would paint en plein air with fellow artists Tan Choh Tee and Lim Tze Peng; frequenting the Botanic Gardens and Mandai Gardens to paint his favourite orchids. Hou’s orchid paintings draw on the principles of ‘xieyi’ (freehand). Hou sought to define his form of expression and painting style with simplicity and elegance; reflecting the essence of subject, stroke and spirit in his harmonious compositions.

Born in China in 1949, Tan Kian Por graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1970 and has studied Chinese ink painting under Shi Xiangtuo and Chen Chong Swee. Influenced by Shanghai school artists Wu Changshuo and Ren Bonian, Tan’s thematic interests include traditional genres of bird and flower paintings although figurative works form a significance portion of his artistic output. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2001.

Born in China in 1949, Tan Kian Por graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1970 and has studied Chinese ink painting under Shi Xiangtuo and Chen Chong Swee. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2001.

Born in 1923, Lim Tze Peng is mainly a self-taught artist. Ink paintings and calligraphic works are predominantly featured in his work, though there is a small collection of early oils, watercolours and drawings. He created a unique style of ink painting using rich brush and tonal details, and this approach is often regarded as a manifestation of the Nanyang Style in the ink medium. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2003.Having grown up in a ‘kampong’ (Malay for village), Lim is attracted by the idyllic life and landscape of rural communities. After his retirement in the 1980s, Lim was able to travel widely to paint his favourite spots before urbanization changed the face of the land. His mastery of the ink medium is evident in the delicate lines and well-controlled ink washes.

Born in 1941 in Malaysia, Tay Chee Toh studied at Singapore’s Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts and has a postgraduate degree (Oil Painting) from China. He is the recipient of numerous painting and sculpture awards, and in 1985 Tay was conferred the Government of Singapore’s Cultural Medallion Award.Dating from between 1988 and 1990, the Singapore Art Museum has several of Tay’s watercolour paintings of still lives and flowers. Created during a period of great artistic output and experimentation by the artist, it is almost curious to see Tay returning to realism with these delicate studies on paper. Like many artists of his generation who passed through the Academy, Tay’s observational and drawing skills are finely honed. These formal academic watercolours appear to present themselves as testimonials, as if the artist has temporarily removed himself from the frenzy of abstract activity to re-focus on the coordination of hand and eye.

Born in China in 1949, Tan Kian Por graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1970 and has studied Chinese ink painting under Shi Xiangtuo and Chen Chong Swee. Influenced by Shanghai school artists Wu Changshuo and Ren Bonian, Tan’s thematic interests include traditional genres of bird and flower paintings although figurative works form a significance portion of his artistic output. He was awarded the Cultural Medallion in 2001.

Born in 1943 in Singapore, Ang Ah Tee graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1962, specializing in Western painting. A versatile artist, Ang is proficient in watercolour, oil and acrylic. Since 1980, he has had more than 10 solo exhibitions, and participated in numerous group exhibitions in Singapore and internationally. He was the recipient of the National Day Art Exhibition Award (1978) and the Cultural Medallion (2009), awarded by the government of Singapore. Ang, who began painting Singapore’s prominent landmarks in the 1970s, would start each new work by first making sketches on location before developing and completing it in his studio. ‘Singapore Scene’ depicts the late 19th-century government building along the Singapore River that now houses the Asian Civilisations Museum. Painted in Ang’s preferred medium of acrylic which allows for the delicacy of watercolour and the richness of oil, the scene is largely executed with transparent washes save for the opacity of the large tree.

Born in 1943 in Singapore, Ang Ah Tee graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1962, specializing in Western painting. A versatile artist, Ang is proficient in watercolour, oil and acrylic. Since 1980, he has had more than 10 solo exhibitions, and participated in numerous group exhibitions in Singapore and internationally. He was the recipient of the National Day Art Exhibition Award (1978) and the Cultural Medallion (2009), awarded by the government of Singapore. Ang, who is known for his lyrical interpretations of Singapore’s urban landscape, depicts the old with the new in ‘Singapore Scene’ by juxtaposing the old shophouses that line the once important Singapore River with the OCBC Centre, the tallest skyscraper in Southeast Asia in the late 1970s.

Born in 1932 in Hainan, China, Aw Tee Hong grew up in Malaysia before settling in Singapore. A graduate from Singapore’s Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1954 and the Beijing Central Academy of Arts & Design in 1958, Aw has held over 21 solo art exhibitions around the world since the early 1970s. A second generation Nanyang artist with a great passion for nature, Aw is conversant in oil painting, Chinese ink, watercolour, mural painting and sculpture.

Born in 1932 in Hainan, China, Aw Tee Hong grew up in Malaysia before settling in Singapore. A graduate from Singapore’s Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1954 and the Beijing Central Academy of Arts & Design in 1958, Aw has held over 21 solo art exhibitions around the world since the early 1970s. A second generation Nanyang artist with a great passion for nature, Aw is conversant in oil painting, Chinese ink, watercolour, mural painting and sculpture.

One of the most prolific and talented artists in Singapore, Ng Eng Teng produced more than 300 sculptures over a span of 40 years. These included large-scale public sculptures to life-sized and smaller sculptures. His art education started in Singapore as a painting student at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), before continuing his studies in industrial pottery in North Staffordshire College of Technology, England. He went on to study studio pottery in Farnham School of Art and subsequently became a designer in a pottery factory in Ireland for a few years. These various stints equipped him with the necessary technical vocabulary to deal with the 3-dimensional form.Ng, who dealt primarily with the human figure and humanistic themes in his art, believed that humanity began with the family foundation and the dynamic relationships between the parents and their children. ‘Mother and Twins’ is another example of his long-standing investigation into the mother-child relationship. Here the anatomy is reduced into an S-shaped curve, reminiscent of the umbilical cord that sustains a child during pregnancy.

Born in 1948 in Singapore, Poh Siew Wah is a self-taught painter and lifelong art teacher. A recipient of several awards, including the Tan Tze Chor Art Award (1987) and a Merit Award, IBM Award (1990), Poh is known for his diverse styles ranging from representational watercolours to abstract paintings.Despite the mid-1980s marking a radical change in Poh’s painting style, from representational to abstract, he did not entirely abandon his trademark watercolour work. In the two watercolour street scenes ‘Little India’ and ‘Mosque’, Poh has impassively recorded the human activity of historic Singapore locations. However, in ‘Door for Calligraphic Hand’ and ‘Red Door at Mohammed Sultan Road’ Poh has zoomed in on specific details of ordinariness, painting close-ups of neglect and of evidence of time passing by. In contrast, this attention focussed on the textured minutiae conveys a nostalgic emotion lacking in the wide-angle views.

Born in 1946 in Malaysia, self-taught painter and sculptor Thang Kiang How’s talent and community service is variously recognised, including: Singapore’s Public Service Medal (1981), Dr Tan Tsze Chor Award (1982), and Special Award, UOB Painting of the Year competition (1985, 1986 and 1987). Exhibiting nationally and internationally, Thang was also President of the Modern Art Society Singapore (1981-93).Following his second solo exhibition in 1989, held at Singapore’s National Museum Art Gallery, Thang continued painting his “Galaxies’ series that then expanded into the “Second Life of Galaxies” series. As with his other ‘Galaxies’ works, this second series consists mostly of square canvasses or sheets of paper with acrylic and compressed air detailing. However, a significant compositional development in the series occurred following an incident with a destructive dog – a completed painting is cut into strips then reconfigured, for example as with ‘Galaxy 228’ and ‘Galaxy Series No. 231’.

Born in 1937 in Indonesia, Goh Beng Kwan migrated with his family to Singapore in 1945. In the 1950s, he studied Chinese ink painting with Chen Wen Hsi. In 1963, he studied at the Art Students' League in New York where he was first introduced to collage art and Abstract Expressionism. Goh was awarded the inaugural UOB Painting of the Year First Prize in 1982 and the Cultural Medallion in 1989.Goh is noted for his collage works with contain an “oriental perspective”. Inspired by nature, environment and his ethnic Chinese heritage, his works relate to sensory impressions of the places he has visited. Goh’s works are mainly mixed media pieces which use natural and man-made materials.

Born in Singapore, Chew Piak San (b. 1942) is a watercolourist and an alumnus of Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts. He is also a member of the Singapore Watercolour Society. A veteran in the Singapore arts scene, Chew has been participating in exhibitions since 1972, including international exhibitions such as the Seoul Olympics Games International Watercolour Exhibition in 1988. Chew has also held exhibitions overseas such as in Malaysia, China, Brunei, Japan and USA. Chew’s paintings of street scenes, nature and flora, which are recurring themes in his artistic oeuvre, are often sensitive and enveloped in a romantic atmosphere. In ‘Reflections II’, sensitive line-work sketch the tree trunks while suffused foliage and still reflections on the water surface add to the tranquillity.

Born in Singapore, Chew Piak San (b. 1942) is a watercolourist and an alumnus of Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts. He is also a member of the Singapore Watercolour Society. A veteran in the Singapore arts scene, Chew has been participating in exhibitions since 1972, including international exhibitions such as the Seoul Olympics Games International Watercolour Exhibition in 1988. Chew has also held exhibitions overseas such as in Malaysia, China, Brunei, Japan and USA.The luminosity of watercolour is used to great effect in ‘Reflections III’ to create an enchanting environment of tranquillity and stillness.

Born in 1945, Anthony Poon was one of Singapore’s pioneering abstract artists. Graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA) in 1964, Poon pursued art studies at Bynam Shaw School of Art in London and Regional College of Art in Bradford. In 1973, Poon won the UOB Painting of the Year Competition and was bestowed the Cultural Medallion in 1991. In 2002, he won the Excellence Prize at the China International City Exhibition and Symposium in Beijing for his 2006 Olympics sculpture. He passed away at the age of 61.

Born in Singapore, Chew Piak San (b. 1942) is a watercolourist and an alumnus of Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts. He is also a member of the Singapore Watercolour Society. A veteran in the Singapore arts scene, Chew has been participating in exhibitions since 1972, including international exhibitions such as the Seoul Olympics Games International Watercolour Exhibition in 1988. Chew has also held exhibitions overseas such as in Malaysia, China, Brunei, Japan and USA.In this sensitively painted artwork of luxuriant blooms flourishing outside a window the nuances in tonality capture effects of light, highlighting the luminous quality of watercolour. This painting is part of a series on windows, street scenes and flowers.

Born in China in 1934, Lee Boon Wang is one of Singapore’s foremost oil painters. After graduating from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1953, Lee became a full-time artist in 1973. In 1982, Lee was awarded the Singapore Distinction Art Award and in 1989, he was the first Singaporean commissioned to paint the Royal Family of Brunei. Up till 2003, Lee has held seven solo exhibitions, and has participated in seven other group exhibitions in Singapore, New York, Moscow and Brunei.Lee depicts the activities going on in a five-foot way, a sheltered pedestrian walkway which is five feet wide. First seen in the 1822 Raffles Town Plan, the space has been used by small businesses such as eateries and barbers, as seen in this work.

Goh was awarded the inaugural UOB Painting of the Year First Prize in 1982 and the Cultural Medallion in 1989.Goh is noted for his collage works with contain an “oriental perspective”. Inspired by nature, environment and his ethnic Chinese heritage, his works relate to sensory impressions of the places he has visited. Born in 1943 in Singapore, Ang Ah Tee graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1962, specializing in Western painting. A versatile artist, Ang is proficient in watercolour, oil and acrylic. Since 1980, he has had more than 10 solo exhibitions, and participated in numerous group exhibitions in Singapore and internationally. He was the recipient of the National Day Art Exhibition Award (1978) and the Cultural Medallion (2009), awarded by the government of Singapore. Ang, who is known for his lyrical interpretations of Singapore’s prominent landmarks and urban landscape, has also produced many works of back lanes and street corners such as ‘Telok Ayer Street’ which depicts a modest extension at the end of a shophouse row.

One of the most prolific and talented artists in Singapore, Ng Eng Teng produced more than 300 sculptures over a span of 40 years. These included large-scale public sculptures to life-sized and smaller sculptures. His art education started in Singapore as a painting student at Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA), before continuing his studies in industrial pottery in North Staffordshire College of Technology, England. He went on to study studio pottery in Farnham School of Art and subsequently became a designer in a pottery factory in Ireland for a few years. These various stints equipped him with the necessary technical vocabulary to deal with the 3-dimensional form.‘Blue Sky’ is a piece of glazed ceramic work where Ng’s sense of ‘play’ is apparent as two extra spouts have been added to the teapot and the handle deliberately enlarged.

Born in Singapore in 1941, Jolly Koh was brought up in Malacca, Malaysia. After years of studying and lecturing Art, he was awarded a Fullbright Scholarship in 1971 and graduated with a doctorate in art education from the University of Indiana, USA in 1976. While essentially subscribing to the Abstract Expressionist philosophy, he was more interested in investigating colour relationships. Drawn from nature, his works are lyrical colour compositions that delight the senses.

Born in 1945, Anthony Poon was one of Singapore’s pioneering abstract artists. After graduating from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA) in 1964, Poon continued his art studies at Bynam Shaw School of Art in London and Regional College of Art in Bradford. In 1973, Poon won the UOB Painting of the Year Competition and was bestowed the Cultural Medallion in 1991. In 2002, he won the Excellence Prize at the China International City Exhibition and Symposium in Beijing for his 2006 Olympics sculpture. He passed away at the age of 61 in 2006.‘Squatri Purma’ is from Poon’s Shaped Canvas series and the Wave series created during the 1970s. During this period, Poon experimented with Colour Field painting and was interested in exploring the linearity and flatness of the pictorial plane. The basic square shape is replicated and rearranged into multiples of clearly defined and illusionary squares. This is achieved through an intelligent interplay of multiple lines and a monochromatic colour with two alternating values.

Born in 1936, Thomas Yeo graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960.  He then studied in Chelsea School of Art and Hammersmith College of Art and Architecture in London.  Since then, Yeo has participated in more than 100 solo and group exhibitions and in 1984, he was awarded the Cultural Medallion.  From the mid-1970s onwards, Yeo began to adopt the use of collage in greater depth and he often incorporated different varieties of rice paper in one work. Collage was, in his words, “a liberation from the tyranny of the brush”.  In this work, the bands of purple and green at the lower half of the painting mark the divide between a sacred and an earthly realm, which is represented by the warm colours. In the background, a white ‘aura’ over greyish blue emits an ambience of holiness linked to the subject, the tabernacle.

Yusman Aman, born in Batu Pahat, Johore, Malaysia in 1939, is largely a self-taught artist as he had declined a scholarship to the prestigious Ecole des Beaux-Arts in Paris in the early 1960s due to family commitments. While working in Singapore as a commercial artist, he had the opportunity to exhibit regularly alongside other well-known artists and represented Singapore in the Adelaide Festival of Arts in the early 1970s. Yusman returned to Malaysia in the mid-1970s where he became the first local artist to open his own an art gallery.Yusman, who started with figurative paintings before moving on to Lyrical Abstraction, gained prominence when he utilized batik and injected modernist influences. ‘Pollination of the Earth’, featuring board horizontal bands of colour and executed in a combination of acrylic and batik dye, is another bold experiment at reinventing the traditional medium.
Donated by Kussudiardja Bagong. © Butet Kartaredjasa

Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Cheong was a well-travelled artist and had visited regional places such as Bali, Borneo and Sarawak to seek out new subject matter. Here is a portrait of a Kenyah lady, the indigenous people of Borneo. Rendered in Chinese ink, Cheong captures the most characteristic feature of the Kenyah women; their extremely long earlobes, distended with heavy earrings.

Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Cheong was a well-travelled artist and had visited regional places such as Bali, Borneo and Sarawak in search of subject matter. ‘Woman Lying Down’ depicts a Kenyah woman lying on a banana leaf. Although painted in Chinese ink, the composition and pose are reminiscent of Western life studies The Kenyah women of Borneo are famous for their extremely long earlobes, distended by heavy earrings.

In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Painted shortly after his arrival in Singapore in 1946, ‘Indian Men with Two Cows’ reflects Cheong's eagerness to seek out and adopt new motifs for his pictorial composition. In this work, a large tree trunk divides the painting boldly, separating the Indian herdsmen from the massive cows.

Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Cheong is known for his bright and intense palette. Here, the contrasting colours of the foreground and background yet create a mystifying ambience, even evoking anxiety and an uncomfortable feeling.

Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.Cheong was one of the most experimental amongst the pioneer artists; he often astounded the art scene with his avant-garde works. This painting shows influence from Cubism. Although undated, the grid overlay, brown palette and stylization of the human figure date the work from the mid 1950s; ‘Goats, Figures and Jugs’ from 1959, in the SAM collection, shows similar qualities.

Cheong Soo Pieng was born in Amoy, China in 1917. He studied at the Amoy Art Academy and the Xinhua Academy of Fine Arts in Shanghai from 1933 to 1936. In 1946, Cheong settled in Singapore and taught at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts until 1961. Together with fellow artists Chen Wen Hsi, Chen Chong Swee and Liu Kang, they went to Bali, Indonesia in 1952 in search of new inspiration and subject matter. Cheong had a dominant influence on the development of modern art in Singapore and is regarded as one of Singapore’s pioneer artists. His innovative experimentations towards developing his stylistic oeuvre had a great influence on his students, many of whom later became established artists in the region. For his contributions in art, he was awarded the Meritorious Service Medal by the Singapore government in 1962. Cheong passed away in 1983 in Singapore.The composition of ‘Landscape’ is dominated by the massive rock outcrop which dwarfs the houses and the two ladies in the foreground. The central placement of the main subject is a layout that is adopted throughout Cheong’s long artistic career. Although undated, the sarong-clad ladies date this work as a post-1946 one when Cheong moved to Singapore and began to depict the people and landscape of Southeast Asia.

Born in 1936, Thomas Yeo graduated from Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts in 1960. He then studied in Chelsea School of Art and Hammersmith College of Art and Architecture in London. Since then, Yeo has participated in more than 100 solo and group exhibitions and in 1984, he was awarded the Cultural Medallion. Painted before Yeo pursued further studies in United Kingdom, this early watercolour work provides an interesting insight into Yeo’s artistic practice. The layering of translucent interlocking planes and the grid-like structures on the right show a clear influence of cubism and reveal the artist’s lifelong concerns with the depiction of pictorial space and abstraction. Using mainly cool, muted colours on the side, Yeo draws the viewer’s attention to the centre of the painting with warm colours, where the main activity is taking place.
